ground_truth.title: The Top 25 Hosting Trends of 2025, According to Event Planners
ground_truth.author: Rebecca Norris
ground_truth.main_content:
Summer is officially here, and with it, endless potential to host the soiree of your dreams. While classic backyard barbecues, whimsical midsummer parties, and elevated alfresco gatherings are common themes this time of year, there’s always the option to think more creatively to host a curated event that guests will remember long after last call. An easy way to get the ball rolling is to brush up on the top hosting trends of 2025. To help you do so, we chatted with some of the industry’s top event planners to uncover the details that make any party stand out.

“This year, it’s less about showing off and more about creating a sense of ease,” says Beth Helmstetter, founder and creative director of Beth Helmstetter Events. “The best events feel intentional, beautiful, and personal.”

Another way to look at it? Through the attendees’ eyes. “What are your guests going to remember? Hosts want their guests to be comfortable, to be entertained, and to be taken care of,” says celebrity party planner Mindy Weiss, founder of Mindy Weiss Party Consultants. Ahead, peruse 25 hosting trends bound to make your special event that much more memorable.

A Focus on Storytelling
What story do you want your event to tell? “This year, it’s not just about beautiful design, it’s about meaning,” says Estera Events Co-Owner and Lead Planner Erica Vanco. “From invitations to tablescapes, hosts are weaving in personal narratives that connect guests to place, memory, or culture. Whether it's referencing a childhood dish, using heirloom decor for the tablescape, or naming cocktails after shared moments, storytelling transforms an event from stylish to deeply personal.”

Customized Menu and Paper Suites
A memorable way to set the tone for your party is with interactive paper suites, encompassing everything from the invitation and RSVP card to the name care and menu. “We love to enhance guest experience with our paper suites,” says Holloway Events Founder and Lead Designer Cathleen Holloway. “A gorgeous color palette with custom shapes previewing the amazing night ahead. Once, we created an interactive oyster plate menu for each wedding guest, which doubled as a placecard as well. Behind each oyster, [we] listed out each course. Guests are still talking about it to this day!”

Neo-Pastel Palettes with Saturated Accents
When it comes to the color story of your event, you have a few options. Up first, we have modern pastels with a pop of vibrant color. “Gone are the saccharine pastels of the past; 2025 sees the rise of sophisticated, dusty pastels grounded by bold, unexpected accent colors,” says Lara Mahler, founder of The Privilege is Mine. “Think: sage with ochre, dusty lilac with marigold, or powder blue with deep wine.”

A Full Drip Color Story
Party color palettes don’t have to be subdued in 2025. In fact, many hosts are opting for deeply saturated color stories. “Clients aren't being shy about their desired color palettes these days,” Holloway says. “Just like we're seeing in the interior design space, our clients are completely leaning into a ‘full drip,’ almost monochromatic palette, for their weddings.”

Bold Color Mixes
If monochromatic color palettes aren’t your thing, turn to the boldest color mixes of the year. “2025 is all about fearless color—rich shades like red, chocolate brown, punchy pinks, lavender, yellow, cobalt, and chartreuse are dominating design palettes,” says Estera Events Co-Owner and Lead Planner Bianca Hall. “The key to keeping this sophisticated is layering saturated hues with wood tones, metallics, or stone to balance drama with elegance. It’s confident, expressive, and anything but boring.”

A Moment for Chartreuse
If you want to make the biggest impact with a single color selection, Holloway says to opt for chartreuse. “We're seeing chartreuse become more welcomed with each passing day. Our clients love the unexpected juxtaposition it creates in a color palette,” she shares. “A punchy Save the Date? A gorgeous table linen? We're here for it. For a recent wedding, our bride kept reiterating she wanted her wedding to feel ‘cool and punchy with a classic twist.’ We incorporated the chartreuse custom pleated table linen with a traditional supper club lamp to create this ‘new traditional’ mix.”

Creating a Home Away from Home
Expanding on a sense of ease, Weiss says that parties in 2025 are being crafted to look and feel like home, regardless of where they’re actually being hosted. “It’s all about the table settings—we’re designing tables that look like the client’s dining room,” she reveals. “There’s a lot of focus on the details—sometimes we spend an entire week just making tabletop decisions.” The reason? At seated dinners and events, guests spend ample time eating, so the dishes, utensils, glassware, and linens get a lot of face time.

“Everyone cares about the dishes and the details,” Weiss points out. “Choosing the right tablecloth, dishes, flatware, glassware, napkin treatment, place card holder—all of these details are big decisions. The good news is the options are no longer limited. Now, there are great companies like Maison de Carine, Casa de Perrin, and Theoni Collection that specialize in really beautiful tabletop pieces you would have in a home. The products don’t look like rentals, and often they are designer brands. Mixing and matching these elements allows hosts to introduce a color theme and style in a very unique and personalized way.”

For an event that's smaller in scale, Weiss says to “simply open your cabinets and pull out everything. Things do not need to match. They just need to be part of the same story. Don’t mix fine china with a melamine plate, but mix colors and patterns and create interest. The pieces should be cohesive, but the colors can vary.”

Mismatched Glassware
When rummaging through your cabinets (or estate sales while hunting for the perfect party setting assortment), Forbes Functions Founder Cameron Forbes says not to worry about selecting a matching set.

“In 2025, it’s all about personality, not perfection, and mismatched glassware captures that vibe perfectly,” she reveals. “Mixing vintage finds or colorful stems adds relaxed charm and a unique story to your table. It’s also fun and sustainable to thrift glassware, giving beautiful pieces a second life while cutting down on waste.”

Layered Tablescapes
Glasses aren’t the only thing that can be mismatched. The way Helmstetter and Vanco see it, layered tablescapes are the most alluring of all.

“Tables feel less composed and more layered with details to discover,” Helmstetter says. “We love tables that include collected pieces such as linens sourced during travels, inherited silver pieces, or artisan ceramics, all mixed intentionally. The result is a table that feels unexpected, elevated, and personal.”

Vanco agrees. “Minimalism has its place; [in 2025] we’re leaning into a more maximal and deeply layered look,” she says. “Multiple linens, stacked plates, colored glassware, and mixed textures create a collected, editorial feel. Each place setting tells a story, inviting guests to explore and appreciate the thoughtful details.”

To create a layered tablescape at home, Helmstetter says to start with a textile you love. “Then add pieces you’ve sourced over time, like a collection of vases or candlesticks that work well together but aren’t exactly all matching,” she instructs. “Add small elements that elevate the dining experience, such as vintage salt cellars, a silver-plated snack tray, or monogrammed dinner napkins. All will feel extra special and unexpected.”

Garden-Inspired Florals
Once upon a time, grand floral centerpieces towering well above the glassware were a must for memorable parties. Now, low, organic flowers steal the show, Weiss says. “The low organic look is very in, which is also reflective of that residential environment,” she shares. “Even a ballroom is designed to look like you’re at home. And if someone does prefer a tall centerpiece, it’s very simple and not over-the-top.”

When selecting the florals for your party or event, Helmstetter says to look to your garden (or some of our gorgeous flower arrangements) for inspiration. “Arrangements are looser and more natural; think: vines, herbs, seasonal flowers, and even produce,” she reveals. “They’re designed to feel like they came from the landscape rather than a florist's fridge.”

For the perfect garden-inspired floral moment, Helmstetter says to use wide, low vessels. “Build with in-season ingredients and style with unexpected elements like fruit or herbs,” she instructs. “Don’t overwork it. Movement and asymmetry make it feel more interesting and natural.”

Food as Decor
Speaking of fruit and herbs, Forbes says that using food as party table decor is trending for 2025. “Halved grapefruits, leafy lemons, herbs cascading from bowls: These edible elements are becoming as much a part of the tablescape as the florals,” she shares. “It’s visual storytelling through ingredients, and I don’t see it going anywhere.”

To execute the theme, Forbes says to look for produce with character. “Citrus with leaves still on, juicy figs, cherry stems, or bunches of herbs—they add color and movement without feeling forced,” she explains, noting that citrus fruits enhance the party’s aroma, too.
